participants.extracted: Adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus and albuminuria treated in the University of Heidelberg Diabetes Outpatient Clinic. Of 694 evaluated patients, 110 met the inclusion criteria and consented to participate: 53 were randomized to the mindfulness-based stress reduction intervention and 57 to usual care/control. Participants were at high risk for diabetes complications, including cardiovascular and microvascular disease, and were followed immediately postintervention and annually for up to 5 years.

interventions.extracted: The intervention was an adapted Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR) program, an 8-week group-based course using body and meditation practices to increase awareness, openness, and acceptance of internal and external experiences. It was modified to include practices for difficult thoughts and feelings related to diabetes. Participants attended weekly group sessions of 6–10 people and a booster session after 6 months, led by a psychologist and a resident in internal medicine. All participants in both study arms also received standardized usual medical care according to diabetes guidelines through regular outpatient visits.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure albuminuria progression as the primary endpoint, assessed using 24-hour urine collections on 3 consecutive days at baseline, immediately postintervention, and then yearly during 5 years of follow-up. Secondary outcomes included psychological distress and psychiatric comorbidity, measured with the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ) at the same time points; depression, measured with the PHQ-9; stress, measured with the PHQ stress scale; subjective health status, measured with the 12-item Short-Form Health Survey (SF-12), including physical and mental component summary scores; routine blood parameters including HbA1c; blood pressure, assessed by 24-hour ambulatory measurement; mortality; cardiovascular events; and activation of proinflammatory transcription factors, all planned as secondary end points during the 5-year follow-up.
